summary:
The Fall of Nineveh (612 BC) was one of the most dramatically swift collapses in ancient history — the destruction of the Assyrian capital city by a coalition of Babylonians under Nabopolassar and Medes under Cyaxares, ending the Neo-Assyrian Empire that had dominated the Near East for over two centuries. The siege lasted three months; ancient sources describe the city's defenses being breached when the Tigris flooded and undermined the walls. The city was sacked, burned, and its population killed or enslaved.

The fall produced shock across the ancient world. The Hebrew prophet Nahum had predicted Nineveh's destruction with evident satisfaction (Nineveh had destroyed the northern Kingdom of Israel), and his book records the jubilation of those who had suffered under Assyrian domination. Yet the speed of Assyria's total collapse — from the world's dominant superpower to complete irrelevance within a decade — was unprecedented. A remnant Assyrian government held out at Harran until 609 BC before final defeat at [[event:3064|Fall of Assyria]]. The Assyrian homeland was never repopulated to its former density, and its cities — including Nineveh, Nimrud, and Assur — were abandoned ruins for millennia, preserving their archaeological record.
